content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COrVIMISSION
P.O. Box 807
• 328 .1 Street
• Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
• Fax (916) 322-0886
September 16, 1999
Michelle Clesceri
Office of the District Attorney
Felony Projects Unit
Post Office Box 808
Santa Ana, California 92702-0808
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-236
Dear Ms. Clesceri:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the mass mailing provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ During our telephone conversation on September 15,
1999, you indicated to me that you are seeking this advice at the request of Mr. Rackauckas.
QUESTION
May the Orange County District Attorney’s picture appear on pamphlets you would like
to hand out? Are there restrictions on the size and number of his pictures?
CONCLUSION
According to your facts, the method of distribution ofthe pamphlets will not result in the
pamphlet becoming subject to the restrictions of Section 89001.
FACTS
The Orange County District Attorney’s Office has created an outreach program to
educate the different ethnic groups in the county. You plan to distribute pamphlets on issues
concerning law enforcement to these groups. You will not be mailing the pamphlets. You will
be handing them out when you visit and speak with these various groups. The district attorney
will be profiled and a picture or pictures ofhim will appear on the pamphlets. Government funds
will be used to pay for the printing of the pamphlets.
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-99-236
Page 2
ANALYSIS
Section 89001 states that “no newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at public
expense.” Regulation 18901 implements section 89001 and provides that a mailing is prohibited
if all of the following apply:
“(1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means, to the recipient at his or her
residence, place of employment or business, or post office box. For purposes of
this subdivision (a)(1), the item delivered to the recipient must be a tangible item,
such as a videotape, record, or button, or a written document.
(2) The item sent either:
(A) Features an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or
sends the mailing, or
(B) Includes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to an elected
officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mailing, and is
prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the
elected officer.
(3)(A) Any of the costs of distribution is paid for with public moneys; or
(B) Costs of design, production, and printing exceeding $50.00 are paid with
public moneys, and the design, production, or printing is done with the intent of
sending the item other than as permitted by this regulation.
(4) More than two hundrecL substantially similar items are sent, in a single
calendar month, excluding any item sent in response to an unsolicited request and
any item described in subdivision (b).”
Thus, a mass mailing is prohibited if all of the factors set out in Regulation 18901(a) are
met. If all the factors are not met, the mailing would not be a prohibited mass mailing and there
would be no restrictions from placing photographs in the pamphlet, or references to elected
officials.
Featuring or Inclusion ofa Reference to an Elected Officer
(1) If the item features
2 an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or
sends the mailing or (2) if the item includes the name, photograph or any reference to an elected
officer who is affiliated with the agency that produces or sends the mailing, and the item is
prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with the elected officer,
the item would be subject to the restrictions of the mass mailing prohibition. (Regulation
1890 1(a)(2).)
2 “Featuring” includes the inclusion of photographs. (Regulation 1890 1(c)(2).) According to your facts,
you plan to include photographs of the district attorney in the pamphlet.


File No. A-99-236
Page 3
You state that the district attorney will be profiled and that you wish to include
photographs of the district attorney. Since a photograph of the official will be included in the
mailing, this factor is met. In addition, the district attorney is affiliated with the agency that will
be producing or distributing the pamphlet. Therefore, the second part of this test is also met.
Public Moneys
Section 89001 provides that no newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at public
expense. Regulation 18901 (a)(3) specifies that a mass mailing is “sent at public expense” within
the meaning of Section 89001 if either the costs of distribution are paid for with public moneys,
or more than $50.00 in public money is paid for the costs of design or production, and the design
or printing is done with the intent of sending the item. The communications in question will be
prepared and distributed with public funds. Therefore, this factor would be met.
Delivery
Regulation 18901 (a)( 1) restricts items that are mailed or delivered, by any means, to a
person’s home, office or post officç box. (Anaya Advice Letter, No. A-91-215.)
Copies of your pamphlet that are set out for the public to pick up on their own are not
subject to the restrictions of Regulation 18901
(Waggoner Advice Letter, No. A-92-247.)
Therefore, this factor is not met.
Mass Mailings
Finally, Regulation 18901 applies only to a “mass mailing” as defined in the Act. “Mass
mailing” means over two hundred substantially similar pieces of mail, but does not include mail
which is sent in response to an unsolicited request, letter or other inquiry, or any items that fall
within the exceptions set forth in Regulation 18901(b). (Section 82041.5.) Regulation 18901
states that “[ajny item sent is delivered, by any means, to the recipient at his or her residence,
place of employment or business, or post office box....” (Regulation 1890 l(a)(1). Under that
definition, the items would not be considered to be “sent” because the items would be handed out
to members of the public when you visit and speak with various groups. Therefore, this factor
would not be met.
Please note, however, that where an item would otherwise fall within the restrictions of Regulation
18901 because the other factors are met, you should be cautious that the pamphlet is not redistributed by one of the
prohibited means. We would suggest a cover letter be included with any bulk delivery to make clear that the items
are not to be redistributed to persons at their home, places of employment or post office box. (Lavagetto Advice
Letter, No. A-90- 199.)


fi
Summary
File No. A-99-236
Page 4
Based on your facts, and because the pamphlet does not meet all of the criteria listed in
Regulation 18901, distribution of the pamphlets as you describe, will not reu1t in the pamphlet
becoming subject to the restrictions of Section 89001 and therefore is not a prohibited mass
mailing. Please note that this advice is limited to the facts provided to us and only addresses the
mass mailing prohibition. You should be aware that there may be other laws that have not been
addressed in this letter. For example, based on the specific circumstances surrounding tle visits
and the speaking engagements that you mention in your letter, you should be aware of Section
82015 which defines the term “contribution.” Please feel free to write to us asking specific
questions regarding whether or not a contribution may exist.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.
SGC:LD:tls
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
Lynda Doherty
Political Reform Consultant, Legal Division
